source_file: [Day 4v2] Adventure Kit; 30 Days Lost in Space (English_ASR).srt
full_text: Ah welcome my Intrepid Explorer to the grand finale of week one or as some might call it day four I must confess you've perform surprisingly well for a human that is today we're going to add a touch more complexity and a Dash more Brilliance to your drowning NightLight we're fixing the storage room and cockpit lights so you won't have to grope around like a lost mle rat any longer we're still under an ocean you're getting cold and the night is dark and full of scampy we have a problem yes another one the hydroponic system that filters your breathable atmosphere needs more light to photosynthesize soak up your bad breath and turn it into O2 we need to get the rest of these dip switch circuits operating before whatever out there starts looking at us like a can of spaghetti first things first the hardware notice how we've added a couple of LEDs and some extra wires for our dip switch don't start flipping switches just yet though without proper instructions in your code they'll just stare back at you with the cold lifeless gaze of purposeless tech and that brings us to our code our all important guide to technological obedience we're defining six new variables that will tell us which pins are pulling their weight in the circuit in the setup function we'll use the pinMode function to designate switches as inputs and LEDs as outputs finally we'll March into the loop function and create three if statements our conditional Knights and shining armor they'll relay the status of the three dip switches so my Brave Explorer are you ready to turn our ship bright enough to lure all manner of prey and predators in this unexplored ocean I know I'm excited too once you finished wiring upload your new code to the hero and let there be light you know the drill let's turn to some instructional material I was able to gather cast on the computer and you guessed it I've also added this to your mission resources for future reference I'll see you soon hello and welcome back to day four of the 30 days Lost in Space Adventure kit yesterday we were able to successfully connect one of our lights to an LED using the dip switch module however we still have it seems two more lights within the ship that need to be controlled with on and off in order to fully fix all the lighting in our spacecraft So today we're going to be taking what we did yesterday and we're just going to be adding on additional Logic for the additional lights that we had from yesterday most of the logic that we have is pretty similar to what we have done in previous days from yesterday we're just kind of doing it for a couple more pins and a couple more LED outputs so that's all we're going to do today starting off let's do what we normally do we have all these comments if they're daunting you can feel free to close them down and you don't have to look at them now you'll notice that there's one other thing that we've done as well if we scroll down to where we usually Define our pinouts today I'm going to be showing you another way to do the exact same thing and this is the way that we're going to be doing it going forward in the past in previous days we used a pound define statement to share what pins we are going to be using for our inputs and outputs at the top of our code this is a good way to do it um this is the way that a lot of code has been using it for a lot of time at this point however the way that that is done is currently depreciated in the the Arduino IDE in the Arduino programming language what this means depreciation in code means that it is currently not best practice to continue using it it means that they're no longer going to be supporting that method of doing something and in this case the pound Define has been depreciated it still works it doesn't seem like it will break in any time shape or form in a long long time however if you hover over type Alias bite which is a uint8 type you can Define the pins using a constant meaning it's not going to change bite which is a data type of type uint 8or T and we can say that this is going to be 10 so here's the old way of doing it and here's the way that we're going to be doing it going forward if you still want to do pound defines you can and it will work however for good programming sake we're going to be using using the non- depreciated method which is going to be using bytes which is of Type U int 8or T you're wondering what the heck that is don't worry too much about it it's simply a data type of what you would think would be 8 Bits And the reason why it we want to use a bite of this is because if you go to pinMode and you go into this function here and you hover over it you'll see that the input it wants is of type uint 8 _ t pin and you int 8or T mode what that means is we're simply just giving this function when it calls it what it wants so there's no confusion with the compiler it doesn't have to do any unnecessary conversions and Etc so if you use Define I believe it does the exact same thing and there should be no issues but just for to keep for depreciation sake we're gonna be going forward and so if you see const bite and then whatever pin we're defining up at the top of our code in future videos you can think of it as the exact same thing functionality wise is doing a pound defined statement now with that out of the way let's go out of let's make sure that we close all these extra comment blocks just to make sure that there's no additional confusion we also have at the top the included libraries if you're using the Arduino ID and you just accidentally happen to erase that nothing will change as the AR we know IDE automatically adds that anyway but it's good just to have it anyway so you know that it's being added automatically if we go down here we can go to our very first line of code and the very first bit of code that we have is defining our pinouts now instead of using the pound Define we're going to be doing const bite cabin lights pin is equal to 10 and now we have two additional ones for each of the extra two lights we have the lights for our storage Bay and our cockpit light lights so we have constant constant simply means that it is exactly that it is constant it will not change if you try to change this variable let's just say you go down to the loop and you want to write something like storage lights pin equals storage lights pin plus one and you want to add one to it essentially trying to make it 12 or whatever this constant will throw out an error and it will say hey buddy you can't do that it's a constant it's got to stay the same by putting constant it just enforces and makes sure that this is not going to change and we don't want it to because this is how we're wiring it up down here below once we go down to the dip switch pins the first pin on the dip switch the second pin on the dip switch and the third pin on the dip switch and we're wiring it to pin two 3 and four respectively you'll notice that this is very very much the same we've just added this here for the extra two switches and we've added these here too for the extra two lights you'll notice the same same is going down for the most of the code Below in the setup we simply say that the cabin lights are going to be an output the storage lights are going to be an output and now the cockpit lights are also going to be an output we just added two more outputs down here and then we have the following we have cabin lights is going to be the input on the dip switch and then we just added the simple um storage light and cockpit lights is going to be the additional inputs for the extra two pins that we're going to be wiring up today if you go down to void Loop you'll notice that we have remember the exact same code as yesterday but instead of written once it's written three times so if the first switch is in the on position then we want to turn that corresponding light on else we want to keep it off same exact scenario for the second pin and same same exact scenario for the third pin and with that we have the finished code for day four simply taking what we did yesterday and writing it a couple extra times for the extra pins and that's about it welcome to the wiring example video for day four today we'll be expanding on what we built on day three using mostly the same ideas but increasing our capability to be able to handle three independent lighting circuits since we are pretty closely repeating what we did last time there's not a lot that is new to explain as before our breadboard contains two separate circuit areas that are used to handle on the leand side the hero input and on the right hand side the LED output functions the difference here being that now each of these two areas now includes three separate channels rather than just one we can see that on the input side here on the left we are now using all three channels of the dip switch module we also now have a common 5V bus that is being used along the the lower red line here being used to supply 5 volts to each of the three channels of the dip switch each of those channels feeds through to its respective 10 kilohm limit resistor here before each of those resistors then connects to a Common Ground bus supplied by this black wire from the hero each of those three channels in addition to feeding through the 10 kiloohm resistor connects with a separate wire to each of the input pins on the hero so now we have three of these signal wires a brown wire a white wire and a yellow wire which in turn connect to pins 2 3 and four as those are the pins specified as input in our software sketch switching over to the output side we now have three output LEDs a red a blue and a green and those will be controlled by our three switches 1 two and three each LED is connected on its low side to this common ground bus that we provided and we're using uh on the on the input side we can see that there are three black jumper wires connected from ground to the low side of our three LEDs finally our red blue and green LEDs are connected to each of the three output pins on the hero so we have an orange wire connected here to the red LED we have a blue wire connected on its positive side on the positive side of the LED also to the hero and finally a green wire and those are connected to pins 10 11 and 12 also consistent with what's in our software sketch so that pretty much completes our setup for the day three circuit so let's go ahead and upload the sketch and try it out so we'll load the sketch and now that the sketch is loaded it should be running we have all of the switches in the off position right now so we wouldn't expect to see anything so we'll go ahead and we'll uh put switch one into the on position and sure enough just as we had expected from day three the red LED comes on but now I can have any combination of the three switches on or off so for example I could put switch three into on and get green to come on or I can have all three of them on together or I can turn number one off and so on so as you play around with this you'll be able to confirm that the logic in our software sketch and the wiring that we've put together here with the hero and our breadboard uh agree with one another and have the desired functionality so uh that's all for today's exploration next after we take a day off for our creative activity we'll be returning on day six to introduce a new type of sensor component into our repertoire and so we'll see you then ah you're back that was barely enough time to even begin missing you well congratulations dear Explorer on Surviving in the first week of your unanticipated yet thoroughly avoidable Sea Adventure you've turned this sunken wreck from a murky cave into a well-lit tin can you're proving quite resourceful for an oxygen breather Bravo or whatever it is your kind says now that you have your lights it seems that I can see into your storage Bay with Newfound Clarity my sensors are picking up a solar array that was previously hidden in the murkiness I must admit I didn't notice it earlier but then again was darker than the depths of a super massive black hole in there let's hold on to that glimmer of hope the hydroponic system is getting up to speed and I've altered the resistances on the new lights so wiring up an LED doesn't turn this ship into the largest underwater Fireball in history rest up Explorer you've done some great work and it looks like we're out of immediate damage
